behaviors.description: Generated function signature matches the expected interface (parameter names, types, return type) from the description
behaviors.justification: Whether a real LLM correctly interprets the description and generates a matching signature is non-deterministic. A mock LLM can always return the 'correct' signature, hiding real failures where different LLMs or prompts produce parameter names, types, or return types that don't match. The signature parsing itself is deterministic, but what gets generated depends on real LLM behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Call a real LLM with a function description specifying exact parameter names, types, and return type. Verify the generated code's signature matches by parsing the AST or function inspection. Repeat with multiple descriptions to expose variance in how the LLM interprets interface requirements. A mock LLM returning a fixed 'correct' signature would not catch cases where real LLMs misinterpret the spec.

behaviors.description: Generated implementation passes all provided test cases on the first or a subsequent attempt (after refinement)
behaviors.justification: Real failures stem from the LLM generating code that doesn't work, even after refinement attempts. A mock LLM that always generates syntactically correct, test-passing code would mask the real failure: that actual LLMs often produce buggy implementations that require multiple refinement cycles. While code execution (System) is necessary infrastructure, the core non-determinism comes from LLM generation quality, and mocking the LLM would hide all real failures in the refinement loop's effectiveness.
behaviors.test_description: For a function that a baseline LLM typically fails to implement correctly in one shot, execute the full generate-test-refine loop with real LLM calls and real code execution (sandboxed subprocess). Verify that after 1–3 refinement attempts, tests pass. Document which attempt(s) the real LLM succeeds on. A mock LLM returning passing code immediately would not demonstrate the refinement loop actually works or that the LLM can improve.

behaviors.description: When tests fail, the refinement step includes failure messages in the next LLM prompt so the model can learn from mistakes
behaviors.justification: This behavior tests a deterministic aspect of the refinement pipeline: does the code that constructs the next prompt correctly extract and include failure messages? Real failures here are functional bugs (dropped messages, wrong format, truncation) not LLM behavior variance. You can mock the LLM's second call while using real test execution to generate real failure messages, then verify the prompt construction logic. No non-determinism is involved; the test is entirely about the control flow and string/data handling between test failure and LLM prompt assembly.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the initial LLM call to return code that fails known test cases. Execute the code against real tests to generate actual failure output. Capture the prompt constructed for the second LLM call (either via mock interception or by mocking the LLM.send method). Verify the prompt includes the exact failure messages from the test runner, not truncated or reformatted. Test edge cases: empty failure message, very long messages, special characters. A pure unit test of prompt assembly would miss integration bugs between test runner output and prompt builder.

behaviors.description: Service stops after 3 failed attempts and returns a structured error with all three attempts and their test results
behaviors.justification: This tests the iteration-count logic and error response structure, both deterministic. Real failures: wrong attempt limit, missing test results in response, response format violations. You can mock both the LLM (to always fail) and code execution (to always fail) while verifying the loop counter and response builder work correctly. No subprocess, OS, or LLM non-determinism is required; the test is entirely about state management and response construction across multiple modules.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the LLM to always return code that fails tests; mock test execution to always return a specific failure. Call the service and let it iterate all 3 times. Verify: (1) loop stops at attempt 3, not 2 or 4; (2) response structure includes all 3 attempts; (3) each attempt includes test results (output, error count, failure messages); (4) response is valid JSON/structured format matching API contract. Test boundary cases: what if LLM fails to return code (malformed)? What if test execution crashes?

behaviors.description: Full loop — generate, test, refine, test again — produces a working implementation for a function that a single LLM call typically gets wrong
behaviors.justification: This behavior encompasses the entire agentic pipeline: real LLM generates suboptimal code, real tests fail, real LLM is given failure context and refines, code is re-tested. Each LLM invocation is non-deterministic; the number of iterations needed varies. Real failure: the refinement loop doesn't improve the code enough to pass tests after 3 tries. This can't be tested at Agentic (single LLM call) because the behavior requires multiple sequential LLM interactions with feedback, making it a full workflow. Mocking either the LLM or test execution would eliminate the test's value.
behaviors.test_description: Select a coding problem known to fail for a baseline LLM on first attempt (e.g., complex string manipulation, multi-step logic). Run the full service with real LLM and real code execution. Verify: (1) first LLM attempt produces code that fails tests; (2) second attempt (with failure context in prompt) improves; (3) by attempt 3 (if reached), tests pass. Verify the failure messages are included in each refinement prompt. Measure: how many real LLM calls were needed, what was the structure of the refinement prompts. Run this multiple times to capture variability in LLM behavior. A test with mocked LLM or test execution would not verify the actual refinement loop effectiveness.
